title: Schedule
text:
All sessions run 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT via Zoom. Each day includes a lecture portion and a hands-on lab. Office hours are 12:00–12:30 PM daily after class.
Lecture slides and lab materials will be posted on the course website before each session. Lecture recordings will be posted after each class.


Day 1 — Monday

Introduction to GIS
Lecture Topics

What is cartography and why it matters for public health
Geographic Information Systems: components, capabilities, and applications
Data models and representation: vector (points, lines, polygons) vs. raster (grids, images)
Attribute data types: nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio — with public health examples
The public health data hierarchy: address, block, block group, tract, ZCTA, county

Lab Exercise

Lab 1: Introduction to ArcGIS Pro — Orientation to ArcGIS Pro through Philadelphia public health data. Create three maps: green water infrastructure near flood zones, heat vulnerability index by census tract, and cooling centers in relation to heat vulnerability. Practice adding data layers, changing symbology, creating layouts, and exporting PDFs.

Readings: Bolstad Ch. 1 (Introduction); Dent Ch. 1 (Cartography)
Go to Day 1 session →



Day 2 — Tuesday

Projections & Data Entry
Lecture Topics

Geodesy: the shape of the Earth and why it complicates mapping
Map projections: types of distortion, projection families, and how to choose
Coordinate systems: geographic (lat/lon) vs. projected (meters/feet)
Practical CRS decisions for public health work (State Plane, UTM, WGS84)
Data entry and editing: on-screen digitizing, GPS data, and field data collection

Lab Exercises

Lab 2a: Projecting Geographic Data — Work with map projections in ArcGIS Pro. Compare the same geographic features displayed in different projections, observe how area and distance measurements change, and practice reprojecting data between coordinate systems.
Lab 2b: Digitizing Data — Create new spatial features through on-screen digitizing. Create point, line, and polygon features and attach attribute information.

Readings: Bolstad Ch. 3 (Projections); Monmonier Ch. 2 (Projections)
Go to Day 2 session →



Day 3 — Wednesday

Data Management & Spatial Analysis
Lecture Topics

Data management and processing: file formats, geodatabases, data sources
Key public health data sources: Census/ACS, CDC PLACES, SVI, EPA, OpenDataPhilly, PASDA
Attribute table operations: queries, joins, relates, field calculator
SQL and Boolean expressions for data selection
Vector spatial analysis: buffers, overlay operations (intersect, clip, union, erase)
Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) and ecological fallacy

Lab Exercises

Lab 3a: Digital Data and Table Operations — Work with common digital data sources. Practice downloading and adding data from national repositories, performing table joins, running queries, and calculating new fields.
Lab 3b: Spatial Analysis — Buffering and Overlay — Apply buffering and overlay operations. Create fixed-distance and variable-distance buffers, perform overlay operations to identify areas meeting multiple spatial criteria.

Readings: Bolstad Ch. 7 (Digital Data); Ch. 9 (Spatial Analysis)
Go to Day 3 session →



Day 4 — Thursday

Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster
Lecture Topics

Network analysis: routing, service areas, closest facility, allocation
Geocoding: converting addresses to coordinates, match quality, and privacy considerations
Geocoding and PHI: Census geocoder, address suppression, cell suppression for published maps
Raster spatial analysis: reclassification, map algebra, zonal statistics
Kernel density estimation for hot-spot mapping

Lab Exercises

Lab 4a: Network Analysis — Perform network-based analyses including shortest route, closest facility, and service area generation.
Lab 4b: Geocoding — Geocode address data and evaluate match quality. Discuss privacy and PHI considerations when mapping individual-level health data.
Lab 4c: Raster Analysis — Work with raster data: reclassify land cover, perform zonal statistics to summarize raster values within polygon boundaries.

Readings: Bolstad Ch. 10 (Raster Analysis); Network Analysis tutorial (provided)
Go to Day 4 session →



Day 5 — Friday

Spatial Estimation & Map Design
Lecture Topics

Spatial estimation and interpolation: nearest neighbor, IDW, kriging
Spatial autocorrelation: Moran’s I, LISA, Getis-Ord Gi*
GIS data visualization and map design: classification methods, color theory, layout principles
Choropleth mapping: equal interval, quantile, natural breaks, standard deviation
Map design for public health communication
Public health GIS applications: case studies and real-world examples

Lab Exercises

Lab 5a: Interpolation — Apply interpolation methods (IDW, kriging) to create continuous surfaces from point data. Compare methods and assess output quality.
Lab 5b: Cartographic Modeling — Build a multi-criteria spatial model combining several data layers. Design a professional map layout communicating the results.

Readings: Bolstad Ch. 12 (Interpolation); Brewer Ch. 4 (Color Basics)
Go to Day 5 session →

title: Software Setup
text: Please install your preferred GIS software before the first class session. If you run into trouble, reach out to the instructors beforehand — we will also set aside time on Day 1 to help with any remaining installation issues.

title: Software Setup
text:
Option 1: ArcGIS Pro (Primary)
ArcGIS Pro is the primary software used in this course. It runs on Windows only — Mac users will need to use a virtual machine (see below).

System Requirements

2-core CPU minimum (4-core recommended)
8 GB RAM minimum (16 GB recommended)
32 GB free disk space
Windows 10 or Windows 11

Full requirements: ArcGIS Pro System Requirements


Installation

You will receive an email from Esri inviting you to set up your ArcGIS Online organizational account. Follow the instructions in that email.
Download ArcGIS Pro from pro.arcgis.com
Install and sign in with your organizational account credentials
Verify the installation by opening ArcGIS Pro and creating a new Map project







NoteTemporary License



If needed, a temporary Esri license will be provided for the duration of the course. However, if your institution has a site license, we recommend using that for uninterrupted access after the course ends.




Mac Users
ArcGIS Pro requires Windows. Mac users have several options:
Parallels Desktop (recommended for Apple Silicon Macs):

Parallels is the best option for M1/M2/M3/M4 Macs and works well with Intel Macs too
Costs about $50/year for an academic license, or $99 standard
Install Windows 11 (ARM for Apple Silicon) within Parallels, then install ArcGIS Pro
parallels.com

VMware Fusion:

Free and paid versions available for both Intel and Apple Silicon Macs
vmware.com/products/fusion

Boot Camp (Intel Macs only):

Partition your Mac to dual-boot into Windows
Free but requires restarting to switch between macOS and Windows
Not available for Apple Silicon Macs

Virtual Remote Desktop:

If none of the above options work, contact the instructors — we can provide temporary access to a virtual desktop with ArcGIS Pro pre-installed







ImportantPhiladelphia City Employees



Based on prior experience, Philadelphia city employees may need IT approval to install ArcGIS Pro on work computers. This can take a day or more, so please submit your IT request well before the course starts.

title: Software Setup
text:
Option 2: QGIS (Open-Source Alternative)
QGIS is a free, open-source GIS that runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux. All course lab exercises are available in QGIS versions. While QGIS does not have every feature found in ArcGIS Pro, it handles the core GIS operations covered in this course.

Installation

Go to qgis.org/download
Download the Long-Term Release (LTR) for your operating system
Install using the default settings
Verify the installation by opening QGIS and creating a new project

QGIS is a good choice if:

You use a Mac and do not want to set up a virtual machine
Your institution does not have an Esri license
You want a free tool you can continue using after the course

title: Software Setup
text:
Additional Software (Optional)
These tools are not required but may be demonstrated or referenced during the course:

GeoDa — Free software for exploratory spatial data analysis. Particularly useful for Moran’s I, LISA cluster maps, and spatial regression. Available for Windows, Mac, and Linux.
GRASS GIS — Another open-source GIS with advanced raster analysis capabilities. Available for Windows, Mac, and Linux.

title: Software Setup
text:
Verifying Your Installation
Before Day 1, confirm that you can:

Open ArcGIS Pro (or QGIS)
Create a new project
Add a basemap layer
Pan and zoom around the map

If any of these steps fail, contact the instructors at daq26@drexel.edu.

title: Software Setup
text:
Downloading Lab Data
Lab data for each day will be available on the course website (learn.dcollege.net) before each session. Download the data to a local folder on your computer — do not work directly from a USB drive, cloud drive, or network drive, as this can cause performance issues with ArcGIS Pro.

title: Software Setup
text:
Deploying This Course Website (Instructor Notes)
This course website is built with Quarto and can be deployed to GitHub Pages:

Install Quarto from quarto.org/docs/get-started
Preview locally:
cd course-website
quarto preview
Render the site:
quarto render
This generates the HTML files in the docs/ folder.
Deploy to GitHub Pages:

Create a GitHub repository (e.g., intro-gis-ph)
Push the course-website/ folder contents to the repository
In the repository Settings > Pages, set the source to the docs/ folder on the main branch
The site will be available at https://[username].github.io/intro-gis-ph/

Update the site: Edit .qmd files, re-render, commit, and push.

For more details, see the Quarto GitHub Pages documentation.

title: Day 3: Data Management & Spatial Analysis
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 3: Data Management & Spatial Analysis
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 3: Data Management & Spatial Analysis
text:
Lecture Topics

Data Management and Processing
Spatial analysis depends on having clean, well-organized data. This section covers how to find, acquire, and manage the data that public health GIS work depends on.
Key public health data sources:

U.S. Census Bureau / ACS — demographics, income, housing, poverty, health insurance at tract and block-group level. Accessed via data.census.gov or NHGIS.
CDC PLACES — small-area estimates for 36 health measures (asthma, diabetes, mental health, preventive services) at census tract and ZCTA level. The single most useful national public health spatial dataset. cdc.gov/places
CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) — composite index of social vulnerability at the tract level, organized into four themes: socioeconomic status, household characteristics, racial/ethnic minority status, and housing type/transportation. atsdr.cdc.gov/placeandhealth/svi
EPA EJScreen — environmental justice screening tool with block-group-level environmental and demographic indicators. epa.gov/ejscreen
OpenDataPhilly — Philadelphia’s open data portal with parks, schools, crashes, 311 calls, health department data, and more. opendataphilly.org
PASDA — Pennsylvania’s spatial data clearinghouse. Boundaries, environmental layers, transportation. pasda.psu.edu

Data management concepts:

File formats: shapefile, file geodatabase, GeoJSON, CSV, GeoTIFF
Attribute tables: structure, field types, primary keys
Table joins: linking tabular data (e.g., health indicators) to geographic boundaries using a shared identifier (typically FIPS GEOID)
Data quality: completeness, accuracy, currency, consistency



Vector Spatial Analysis
Vector spatial analysis is the core toolkit for most public health GIS work. Buffers and overlay operations let you answer questions like “what population lives near this facility?” and “where do two risk factors overlap?”
Buffers:

Fixed-distance buffer — same distance around every feature (e.g., 1 km around all TRI facilities)
Variable-distance buffer — distance varies by an attribute (e.g., buffer based on facility emission volume)
Multi-ring buffer — concentric zones (e.g., 0–500 m, 500 m–1 km, 1–2 km)
Dissolve vs. no-dissolve — whether overlapping buffers merge or stay separate

Overlay operations:

Intersect — keeps only the area shared by both layers
Union — combines all areas from both layers
Clip — trims one layer to the boundary of another
Erase — removes the area of one layer from another

Selection:

Select by attribute — SQL-based queries on the attribute table (e.g., POVERTY_RATE > 0.25)
Select by location — spatial queries based on proximity, containment, or intersection (e.g., select all tracts that intersect a flood zone)







NoteMAUP and Ecological Fallacy



The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP) occurs when the same data produces different results depending on how geographic boundaries are drawn. The ecological fallacy is the error of inferring individual-level relationships from aggregate data. Both are common pitfalls in public health spatial analysis — for example, a tract-level correlation between poverty and asthma prevalence does not prove that poor individuals have higher asthma rates.

title: Day 3: Data Management & Spatial Analysis
text:
Lab Exercises

Lab 3a: Digital Data and Table Operations
What you will learn:

Find and download data from national repositories
Add tabular data to a GIS project
Perform table joins (linking health data to census geography by GEOID)
Query attribute tables using SQL/Boolean expressions
Use the field calculator to compute new variables
Generate summary statistics

What you will produce:

A population density map from census data with selected cities
A proportional symbol map of county population
A shaded relief map with hydrography overlay
A wetlands map classified by size

Data: Census county data, U.S. cities, USGS watershed boundaries, National Elevation Dataset, National Hydrologic Dataset, wetland boundaries
Skills practiced:

Downloading and integrating data from multiple sources
Table joins by shared key field
SQL queries and Boolean selections
Field calculator operations
Proportional symbol and graduated color symbology



Lab 3b: Spatial Analysis — Buffering and Overlay
What you will learn:

Create fixed-distance and variable-distance buffers
Perform overlay operations (intersect, union, erase)
Combine spatial operations to answer multi-criteria questions
Calculate areas and proportions for spatial analysis results

What you will produce:

A map of lake variable-distance buffer zones
A map identifying suitable recreation areas based on multiple spatial criteria (distance from roads, proximity to lakes, within public land)
A map of suitable recreation areas on private land only (using erase)

Data: Lakes, roads, and public land boundaries (UTM Zone 15, NAD83, meters)
Skills practiced:

Fixed-distance buffering
Variable-distance buffering (using an attribute field)
Multipart to singlepart conversion
Union and erase overlay operations
Area calculation and spatial query

title: Day 3: Data Management & Spatial Analysis
text:
Readings
Required:

Bolstad & Manson, GIS Fundamentals, Chapter 7: Digital Data
Bolstad & Manson, GIS Fundamentals, Chapter 9: Vector Spatial Analysis

Supplemental:

Dent, Cartography, Chapter 12
Openshaw (1984), “Ecological Fallacies and the Analysis of Areal Census Data”

title: Day 3: Data Management & Spatial Analysis
text:
Key Takeaways
After Day 3, you should be able to:

Identify and download public health spatial data from national and local sources
Join tabular health data to geographic boundaries using GEOID
Apply buffer and overlay operations to answer spatial questions
Recognize when MAUP or ecological fallacy might affect your analysis

title: Day 5: Spatial Estimation & Map Design
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 5: Spatial Estimation & Map Design
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 5: Spatial Estimation & Map Design
text:
Lecture Topics

Spatial Estimation and Interpolation
Interpolation creates a continuous surface from a set of sample points. In public health, this is used to estimate environmental exposures (e.g., air pollution, temperature) at locations where no direct measurement exists.
Common methods:

Nearest Neighbor (Thiessen/Voronoi polygons) — assigns the value of the closest sample point. Simple but creates abrupt boundaries.
Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW) — weighted average of nearby points, with closer points given more weight. Sensitive to clustering of sample points.
Spline — fits a smooth surface through sample points. Good for gently varying surfaces; can produce unrealistic values between sparse points.
Kriging — statistically optimal interpolation that models spatial autocorrelation (the tendency for nearby values to be similar). Provides prediction uncertainty estimates. The gold standard for environmental surfaces, but requires stationarity and enough sample points.

Practical guidance for public health: For most public health applications involving environmental surfaces (PM2.5, temperature, noise), kriging or IDW with appropriate validation is sufficient. For rate stabilization and hot-spot analysis, kernel density or empirical Bayes smoothing (available in GeoDa) is more appropriate than traditional interpolation.


Spatial Autocorrelation
Spatial autocorrelation is the tendency for nearby locations to have similar values — “everything is related to everything else, but near things are more related than distant things” (Tobler’s First Law of Geography). Measuring it is fundamental to spatial epidemiology.
Key concepts:

Moran’s I (Global) — a single statistic summarizing the degree of spatial clustering across the entire study area. Ranges from -1 (perfect dispersion) to +1 (perfect clustering). Values near 0 indicate spatial randomness.
Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA) — identifies where clusters are located. Produces a cluster map showing:

High-High — locations with high values surrounded by high values (hot spots)
Low-Low — locations with low values surrounded by low values (cold spots)
High-Low and Low-High — spatial outliers

Getis-Ord Gi* — identifies statistically significant hot spots and cold spots based on the concentration of high or low values in a neighborhood

Public health applications: Identifying disease clusters, mapping persistent crash hot spots, detecting spatial patterns in health disparities, evaluating whether a health outcome is spatially random or clustered.


GIS Data Visualization and Map Design
The final step in any GIS analysis is communicating results through effective maps. This section covers the principles of cartographic design that make maps clear, accurate, and appropriate for their audience.
Classification methods for choropleth maps:

Equal interval — divides the data range into equal-width classes. Simple but can be misleading if data is skewed.
Quantile — equal number of features in each class. Good for comparing relative rank but can put very different values in the same class.
Natural breaks (Jenks) — minimizes within-class variance. Often the best default for exploratory mapping.
Standard deviation — classes based on distance from the mean. Useful for showing where values are unusually high or low.

Color principles:

Sequential schemes — light to dark for ordered data (e.g., low-to-high prevalence). Use for most public health choropleths.
Diverging schemes — two contrasting hues meeting at a neutral midpoint. Use when there is a meaningful center (e.g., above/below average).
Qualitative schemes — distinct hues for categorical data (e.g., land use types).
Use ColorBrewer to select color schemes that are print-safe and colorblind-safe.
Avoid red-green diverging schemes (colorblind-unfriendly).

Map layout best practices:

Every thematic map needs: title, legend, scale bar, north arrow, data source, date, author
Match the visual hierarchy to the information hierarchy — the most important information should be most visually prominent
Keep it simple: remove unnecessary elements that do not support the map’s message







NoteSmall Numbers and Rate Stability



Mapping crude rates for rare events in small populations produces noisy, unreliable maps. A tract with 2 cases out of 500 people (rate = 400 per 100,000) is not meaningfully different from a tract with 0 cases — it is just random variation. Solutions include using larger geographic units, age-adjusting, applying empirical Bayes smoothing (GeoDa does this), or suppressing tracts with small denominators.




Public Health GIS Applications
The course concludes with real-world case studies showing how GIS has been applied to public health problems, including pedestrian injury analysis, healthcare access mapping, environmental justice screening, and health equity assessment.

title: Day 5: Spatial Estimation & Map Design
text:
Lab Exercises

Lab 5a: Interpolation
What you will learn:

Apply interpolation methods (IDW, kriging) to create continuous surfaces from point measurements
Compare interpolation outputs under different parameters (e.g., IDW power, kriging model)
Assess interpolation quality and identify areas of uncertainty

What you will produce:

Interpolated surfaces using IDW and kriging
Visual comparison of methods
Assessment of prediction quality

Skills practiced:

Configuring interpolation tools (search radius, power/model settings)
Interpreting interpolated surfaces
Understanding the limitations of interpolation with sparse data



Lab 5b: Cartographic Modeling
What you will learn:

Build a multi-criteria spatial model combining several data layers
Apply weighted overlay or raster-based suitability analysis
Design a professional map layout communicating results

What you will produce:

A composite suitability or risk map derived from multiple input layers
A professional map layout with all required elements (title, legend, scale, source, north arrow)

Skills practiced:

Multi-layer analysis workflow
Reclassification and weighting
Map layout design and export
Communicating analytical results through cartography

title: Day 5: Spatial Estimation & Map Design
text:
Readings
Required:

Bolstad & Manson, GIS Fundamentals, Chapter 12: Spatial Estimation (Interpolation)

Supplemental:

Brewer, Designing Better Maps, Chapter 4: Color Basics
Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Chapter 10: Data Maps
Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Chapter 11: Color
Gelman & Price (1999), “All Maps of Parameter Estimates Are Misleading” (Statistics in Medicine)

title: Day 5: Spatial Estimation & Map Design
text:
Key Takeaways
After Day 5, you should be able to:

Apply interpolation methods to create continuous surfaces and understand their limitations
Interpret Moran’s I and LISA cluster maps for spatial pattern detection
Design effective choropleth maps using appropriate classification, color, and layout
Produce a professional map layout suitable for a report or presentation
Recognize the limitations of mapping rare events in small areas

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Urban Health Summer Institute 2026 | Drexel University
A five-day intensive course on Geographic Information Systems for public health professionals

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
Course Overview
This course introduces the fundamental concepts and techniques of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) with a public health focus. Over five days, you will learn to acquire, manage, analyze, and visualize spatial data using ArcGIS Pro and QGIS, with datasets drawn from Philadelphia and national public health sources.
The course is designed for working public health professionals with no prior GIS experience. All lectures and hands-on exercises are completed during class sessions.



Instructor
Alex Quistberg, PhD, MPH
Associate Research Professor
Director, Urban Health MPH Program
Urban Health Collaborative
Dept of Environmental & Occupational Health
Dornsife School of Public Health
Drexel University
daq26@drexel.edu




Schedule
Dates: June 2026 (Mon–Fri)
Time: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT
Format: Via Zoom
Office Hours: 12:00–12:30 PM daily

Course Website:
Blackboard (learn.dcollege.net)
Credit: 1.5 CEU or 15 CPH




Software
ArcGIS Pro Primary platform
Windows required (Parallels for Mac)
QGIS Open-source alternative
Works on Windows, Mac, and Linux
See Setup for installation instructions.
Textbook: GIS Fundamentals (7th ed) by Bolstad & Manson

title: Introduction to GIS for Public Health
text:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this course, you will be able to:

Understand GIS fundamentals — core concepts, data models, and spatial thinking applied to public health problems
Acquire public health spatial data — work with Census/ACS demographics, CDC PLACES health estimates, Social Vulnerability Index, EPA environmental data, and local sources like OpenDataPhilly
Manage spatial data — handle coordinate systems and projections, join tabular health data to geographic boundaries, and maintain data quality
Perform spatial analysis — apply buffering, overlay, network analysis, geocoding, and raster operations to answer public health research questions
Create professional maps — design thematic maps (choropleths, proportional symbols, kernel density) for public health communication
Think critically about spatial data — recognize issues like the modifiable areal unit problem, ecological fallacy, small-number instability in rates, and privacy considerations when mapping health data

Paul Bolstad & Steve Manson
Publisher: Baker & Taylor
ISBN: 978-1-59399-552-2
gisfundamentals.org
The textbook provides background on the concepts covered in lectures. It is a helpful reference for future GIS work, but is not strictly necessary to complete the lab exercises.

Mastering ArcGIS Pro (2nd Edition)

title: Readings
text:
Readings by Day

Day 1 — Introduction to GIS

Day 1 Bolstad & Manson, Ch. 1: An Introduction to GIS — Overview of GIS concepts, components, and applications.


Day 1 Dent, Cartography: Thematic Map Design, Ch. 1: Introduction to Thematic Mapping — Foundations of map design and the role of thematic maps in communication.


Day 1 Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Ch. 1 & 3 — How maps can mislead, and the relationship between scale and generalization.



Day 2 — Projections & Data Entry

Day 2 Bolstad & Manson, Ch. 3: Geodesy, Projections, and Coordinate Systems — The shape of the Earth, map projections, and coordinate reference systems.


Day 2 Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Ch. 2: Projections — How projection choice affects map appearance and interpretation.



Day 3 — Data Management & Spatial Analysis

Day 3 Bolstad & Manson, Ch. 7: Digital Data — Sources and types of digital geographic data.


Day 3 Bolstad & Manson, Ch. 9: Vector Spatial Analysis — Buffering, overlay, and other vector operations.


Day 3 Dent, Cartography, Ch. 12 — Dot and proportional symbol mapping.


Day 3 Openshaw (1984), “Ecological Fallacies and the Analysis of Areal Census Data” — The risks of drawing individual-level conclusions from aggregate spatial data.



Day 4 — Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster

Day 4 Bolstad & Manson, Ch. 10: Raster Analysis — Raster data models, reclassification, map algebra, and zonal operations.


Day 4 Ribeiro et al., “Tools for Address Georeferencing in Public Health” (PLoS ONE) — Review of geocoding tools and accuracy considerations for health research.


Day 4 Network Analysis tutorial (provided) — Step-by-step guide to ArcGIS Pro Network Analyst.



Day 5 — Spatial Estimation & Map Design

Day 5 Bolstad & Manson, Ch. 12: Spatial Estimation — Interpolation methods including IDW, splines, and kriging.


Day 5 Brewer, Designing Better Maps, Ch. 4: Color Basics — Principles of color use in cartography.


Day 5 Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Ch. 10: Data Maps — How classification and symbolization choices affect map interpretation.


Day 5 Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Ch. 11: Color — The role of color in map communication and potential misuse.


Day 5 Gelman & Price (1999), “All Maps of Parameter Estimates Are Misleading” (Statistics in Medicine) — Why visualizing uncertainty matters when mapping estimated parameters.

title: Readings
text:
Additional References
These readings provide deeper background on topics covered in the course. They are optional but recommended for students planning to use GIS in their research.
Spatial Analysis and Epidemiology:

Anselin (2005), “Under the Hood: Issues in the Specification and Interpretation of Spatial Regression Models” (Agricultural Economics)
Baller et al. (2002), “The Interchangeability of Homicide Data Sources: A Spatial Analytical Perspective”
Voss et al. (2006), “Census Exploratory Spatial Data Analysis”
Coombes et al. (1979), “Daily Urban Systems in Britain: From Theory to Practice”

Cartography and Visualization:

Dent, Cartography, Ch. 8 (pp. 131–149) — Map design and visual hierarchy

Geocoding and Address Data:

Nominatim documentation (OpenStreetMap geocoding service)

title: Day 4: Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 4: Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 4: Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster
text:
Lecture Topics

Network Analysis
Network analysis uses the topology of connected line features (roads, transit routes, walking paths) to answer questions about accessibility and routing. This is directly relevant to healthcare access analysis and emergency response planning.
Key operations:

Shortest route — find the optimal path between two locations along a road or transit network
Closest facility — determine which facility (hospital, clinic, fire station) is nearest to a set of demand points
Service area / isochrone — define the area reachable within a given travel time or distance from a facility (e.g., the 15-minute driving catchment of an FQHC)
Allocation — assign demand points to their nearest or most appropriate facility

Public health applications: Identifying populations with poor healthcare access, optimizing facility placement, evaluating transit equity, modeling emergency response times.


Geocoding
Geocoding is the process of converting street addresses into geographic coordinates (latitude, longitude). It is one of the most common GIS operations in public health, used whenever individual-level data includes addresses (disease reports, death certificates, crash reports, patient records).
Key concepts:

Address components: street number, street name, city, state, ZIP code
Match quality: exact match vs. interpolated (placed along a street segment based on address range). Interpolated geocodes can be off by tens of meters, which matters for small-buffer analyses.
Match rate: the percentage of addresses successfully geocoded. Always report this.
Reference data quality: the accuracy of the geocoder’s underlying address database







ImportantGeocoding and Privacy (PHI)



When geocoding health data with personally identifiable information (PHI), you must consider:

Which geocoder to use: The U.S. Census Geocoder is free and does not retain submitted addresses. Cloud-based commercial geocoders (Esri, Google) may store queries — check your IRB protocol and data use agreement.
Address suppression: Never publish maps at the address level for health data. Aggregate to census tract or higher.
Cell suppression: Suppress counts fewer than 5 (or per your agency’s policy) to prevent re-identification.
The analyst’s working copy vs. the published map: You may geocode to rooftop precision for analysis but must aggregate before sharing results.





Raster Spatial Analysis
Raster analysis works with gridded data — continuous surfaces where each cell holds a value. In public health, raster data is used for environmental exposures (air quality, temperature, vegetation), terrain, and density surfaces.
Key operations:

Reclassify — assign new values to raster cells based on ranges (e.g., reclassify land surface temperature into “high,” “medium,” “low” heat categories)
Map algebra — cell-by-cell arithmetic on rasters (add, subtract, multiply, divide layers)
Zonal statistics — summarize raster values within polygon boundaries (e.g., mean PM2.5 concentration by census tract, mean NDVI by ZCTA). This is the single most useful raster operation for public health.
Kernel density estimation — create a continuous density surface from point data (e.g., crash density, disease case density). Controls: bandwidth (search radius) and cell size.

title: Day 4: Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster
text:
Lab Exercises

Lab 4a: Network Analysis
What you will learn:

Set up and use network analysis tools in ArcGIS Pro
Generate shortest routes along a road network
Find the closest facility to a set of locations
Create service areas (isochrones) showing areas reachable within a specified travel time or distance

What you will produce:

Shortest route maps between specified locations
Closest facility analysis results
Service area maps showing travel-time catchments

Skills practiced:

Configuring network analysis layers
Setting impedance (time or distance)
Interpreting network analysis results for accessibility assessment



Lab 4b: Geocoding
What you will learn:

Prepare address data for geocoding (standardization, formatting)
Geocode addresses using ArcGIS Pro or the Census Geocoder
Evaluate geocoding results: match rate, match quality, unmatched addresses
Rematching and manual correction of failed matches

What you will produce:

A geocoded point layer from an address table
A summary of match rates and quality assessment

Skills practiced:

Address table preparation and cleaning
Batch geocoding
Interpreting match scores
Understanding geocode accuracy and its implications for spatial analysis



Lab 4c: Raster Analysis
What you will learn:

Add and display raster data
Reclassify raster values into categories
Perform raster calculations (map algebra)
Extract raster values to points or zones

What you will produce:

A reclassified land cover map
Results of raster-based spatial analysis

Data: Raster datasets including elevation, land cover, and derived surfaces
Skills practiced:

Raster symbology and display
Reclassification
Raster calculator / map algebra
Zonal statistics








TipQGIS Alternative for Network Analysis



QGIS does not include a full network analysis toolset equivalent to ArcGIS Network Analyst out of the box, but the QGIS Network Analysis plugin and built-in shortest-path tools provide basic routing and service area capabilities. A separate QGIS lab guide is provided for Day 4 network and geocoding exercises.

title: Day 4: Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster
text:
Readings
Required:

Bolstad & Manson, GIS Fundamentals, Chapter 10: Raster Analysis
Network Analysis tutorial (provided on course website)

Supplemental:

Ribeiro et al., “Tools for Address Georeferencing in Public Health” (PLoS ONE)
ArcGIS Pro Geocoding Tutorial (provided on course website)

title: Day 4: Network Analysis, Geocoding & Raster
text:
Key Takeaways
After Day 4, you should be able to:

Perform basic network analysis to assess spatial accessibility
Geocode address data and evaluate match quality
Apply appropriate privacy protections when geocoding health data
Perform raster reclassification and zonal statistics
Understand when kernel density is appropriate for public health hot-spot analysis

title: Day 2: Projections & Data Entry
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 2: Projections & Data Entry
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 2: Projections & Data Entry
text:
Lecture Topics

Geodesy, Projections, and Coordinate Systems
The Earth is not flat, and representing its curved surface on a flat map inevitably introduces distortion. This section covers the practical aspects of map projections that matter most for public health work.
The core problem: Flattening a 3D surface onto a 2D map distorts one or more of: shape, area, distance, or direction. Different projections preserve different properties.
Key concepts:

Geographic coordinate systems — latitude and longitude (degrees); the default for GPS data (WGS84). Units are in degrees, not meters, so you cannot directly measure distances or areas.
Projected coordinate systems — coordinates in linear units (meters or feet) on a flat surface. Required for distance and area calculations.
Common CRS choices for public health work in Pennsylvania:

NAD83 PA State Plane South (EPSG:2272) — feet; good for Philadelphia-area work
NAD83 UTM Zone 18N (EPSG:26918) — meters; good for regional analyses
WGS84 (EPSG:4326) — geographic; for GPS data and web mapping

EPSG codes — standardized numeric identifiers for coordinate reference systems. Look up at epsg.io.
On-the-fly projection — ArcGIS Pro can display layers in different CRS together, but this is for display only. Always reproject your data before doing spatial analysis.







ImportantWhen your buffer is wrong



If you run a 1-mile buffer on data in a geographic coordinate system (WGS84), the buffer will be in degrees, not miles. At Philadelphia’s latitude, 1 degree of longitude is about 53 miles. This is one of the most common GIS mistakes in public health research — and it produces results that look plausible but are completely wrong.
Rule of thumb: Always check your CRS before running any distance or area calculation. If your units are in degrees, reproject first.




Data Entry and Editing
Not all spatial data comes ready-made. Sometimes you need to create new features from scratch — for example, digitizing boundaries for a study area or recording observation points from a field audit.
Key concepts:

On-screen digitizing: creating point, line, and polygon features by tracing on a basemap or aerial imagery
Attribute entry: adding descriptive information to each feature you create
Snapping and topology: ensuring that features connect properly (no gaps, no overlaps)
GPS data integration: importing waypoints and tracks from field data collection
Practical applications in public health: digitizing study area boundaries, marking audit observation points, creating intervention zone polygons

title: Day 2: Projections & Data Entry
text:
Lab Exercises

Lab 2a: Projecting Geographic Data
What you will learn:

Identify a layer’s coordinate reference system
Understand the difference between geographic and projected coordinate systems
Reproject data from one CRS to another
Observe how projection affects distance and area measurements

What you will produce:

Maps of Minnesota in multiple projections (Albers Equal Area, Mercator) showing how the same geography looks different in each
A worksheet recording area and distance measurements in different projections, demonstrating measurement distortion

Data:

Minnesota county boundaries and city locations in multiple projections
U.S. lower-48 state boundaries
Graticule (latitude/longitude grid)

Skills practiced:

Setting and changing the map’s coordinate system
Using the Project tool to reproject data
Measuring distances and areas
Comparing measurements across projections
Understanding on-the-fly projection vs. actual reprojection



Lab 2b: Digitizing Data
What you will learn:

Create a new feature class (point, line, or polygon)
Digitize features on screen using a basemap
Add and populate attribute fields
Edit and modify existing features
Calculate geometry (area, length, coordinates)

Skills practiced:

Creating new feature classes with defined schemas
On-screen digitizing with snapping
Attribute data entry
Geometry calculations

title: Day 2: Projections & Data Entry
text:
Readings
Required:

Bolstad & Manson, GIS Fundamentals, Chapter 3: Geodesy, Projections, and Coordinate Systems

Supplemental:

Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Chapter 2: Projections

title: Day 2: Projections & Data Entry
text:
Key Takeaways
After Day 2, you should be able to:

Explain the difference between geographic and projected coordinate systems
Choose an appropriate CRS for a given public health analysis task
Check a dataset’s CRS and reproject it if needed
Create new spatial features through on-screen digitizing

title: Day 1: Introduction to GIS
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 1: Introduction to GIS
text: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM EDT | Lecture + Lab

title: Day 1: Introduction to GIS
text:
Lecture Topics

Introduction to Cartography
Maps are among the oldest forms of communication, and cartography — the art and science of mapmaking — underpins everything we do in GIS. This opening section covers what makes a map effective, how maps encode spatial information, and how cartographic principles apply to public health communication.
Key concepts:

Map elements: title, legend, scale bar, north arrow, data source, projection information
Reference maps vs. thematic maps
Map scale: large-scale (detailed, small area) vs. small-scale (less detailed, large area)
Generalization: how and why we simplify spatial information for display



Geographic Information Systems
A GIS is a system for capturing, storing, analyzing, managing, and presenting spatial data. This section introduces the components of a GIS and why spatial analysis matters for public health.
Key concepts:

The five components of a GIS: hardware, software, data, people, methods
Spatial questions GIS can answer: Where is it? What is nearby? What patterns exist? How has it changed?
Public health applications: disease surveillance, environmental exposure assessment, healthcare access analysis, injury mapping, health equity assessment



Data Models and Representation
GIS data comes in two fundamental forms: vector and raster. Understanding when to use each is essential for choosing the right approach to a public health question.
Key concepts:

Vector data: points (e.g., clinic locations, crash sites), lines (e.g., road networks, transit routes), polygons (e.g., census tracts, park boundaries)
Raster data: continuous grids (e.g., land surface temperature, air pollution surfaces, satellite imagery, elevation)
Attribute data types: nominal (race/ethnicity categories), ordinal (SVI quartile), interval (calendar year), ratio (age-adjusted mortality rate)
Common file formats: shapefile, geodatabase, GeoJSON, GeoTIFF, CSV with coordinates



The Public Health Data Hierarchy
Public health data exists at many geographic scales. Understanding the hierarchy is critical for choosing appropriate units of analysis and avoiding common pitfalls.

Individual address — highest resolution; privacy/HIPAA concerns
Census block — smallest Census unit (~600–3,000 people in urban areas)
Block group — cluster of blocks (~600–3,000 people); ACS data available here
Census tract — designed to be socioeconomically homogeneous (~1,200–8,000 people); most common unit for health data (CDC PLACES, SVI)
ZCTA — ZIP Code Tabulation Area (not the same as ZIP codes); used by some CDC products
County — vital statistics, mortality data, BRFSS

title: Day 1: Introduction to GIS
text:
Lab Exercise

Lab 1: Introduction to ArcGIS Pro
What you will learn:

Start ArcGIS Pro and create a new project
Add data layers from local files
Pan, zoom, and navigate the map
Change data symbology (single symbol, graduated colors, categories)
Create map layouts with legends, titles, north arrows, and scale bars
Export maps to PDF

What you will produce: Three maps of Philadelphia:

Green infrastructure and flood zones — Green water infrastructure projects overlaid on FEMA 100-year flood zones, with census tract boundaries as a base layer
Heat vulnerability index — Census tract-level heat vulnerability scores displayed as a graduated color choropleth with an inset map of a high-vulnerability area
Cooling centers and heat vulnerability — Side-by-side comparison of heat vulnerability and the locations of public swimming pools and spraygrounds as cooling resources

Data:

Philadelphia_census_tracts.shp — Census tract boundaries
GSI_public_projects.shp — Green stormwater infrastructure projects
100_yr_flood.shp — FEMA 100-year flood zone boundaries
heat_vulnerability_ct.shp — Heat vulnerability index by census tract
PPR_spraygrounds.shp — Philadelphia Parks & Recreation spraygrounds
PPR_Swimming_Pools.shp — Philadelphia Parks & Recreation public pools

Skills practiced:

Software orientation and navigation
Adding and ordering data layers
Symbology: single symbol, graduated colors (equal interval, quantile)
Layout creation and element placement
Inset map creation
Distance measurement tools
Exporting to PDF








TipArcGIS Pro and QGIS



All labs are available for both ArcGIS Pro and QGIS. If you are using QGIS, the equivalent operations are: Add Vector Layer, Properties > Symbology > Graduated, and Print Layout for map export.

title: Day 1: Introduction to GIS
text:
Readings
Required:

Bolstad & Manson, GIS Fundamentals, Chapter 1: An Introduction to GIS

Supplemental:

Dent, Cartography: Thematic Map Design, Chapter 1: Introduction to Thematic Mapping
Monmonier, How to Lie with Maps, Chapters 1 and 3

title: Day 1: Introduction to GIS
text:
Preparation
Before Day 1, make sure you have:

ArcGIS Pro installed and working (or QGIS as an alternative)
Downloaded the Day 1 lab data from the course website
Tested that you can launch ArcGIS Pro and create a new project

See the Setup page for detailed installation instructions.

title: Resources
text:
Source
Description
URL




OpenDataPhilly
Philadelphia’s open data portal: parks, schools, crashes, 311, health, vacant lots, zoning
opendataphilly.org


PASDA
Pennsylvania Spatial Data Access — state boundaries, environmental layers, transportation
pasda.psu.edu


DVRPC
Delaware Valley Regional Planning Commission — transportation, land use, equity data for the 9-county region
dvrpc.org/data


PHL Maps
Philadelphia’s ArcGIS Hub — health department data, city services, infrastructure
phl.maps.arcgis.com


PA DOH
Pennsylvania Department of Health — county-level health statistics via EpiQMS
health.pa.gov


PennDOT Crash Data
Motor vehicle crash records for Pennsylvania (processed versions available via DVRPC)
penndot.pa.gov









Source
Description
URL




CDC PLACES
Tract- and ZCTA-level estimates for 36 health measures (asthma, diabetes, mental health, preventive services)
cdc.gov/places


CDC/ATSDR SVI
Social Vulnerability Index at tract and county level — 4 themes, pre-joined to Census geometry
atsdr.cdc.gov/placeandhealth/svi


CDC/ATSDR EJI
Environmental Justice Index — integrates environmental burden with social vulnerability
atsdr.cdc.gov/placeandhealth/eji


EPA EJScreen
Environmental indicators at block group: PM2.5, ozone, traffic, lead paint, hazardous waste
epa.gov/ejscreen


EPA TRI
Toxic Release Inventory — facility-level emissions reporting
epa.gov/toxics-release-inventory-tri-program


HRSA Data
Health Professional Shortage Areas, Medically Underserved Areas, provider locations
data.hrsa.gov


CMS Provider Data
Hospital, FQHC, nursing facility locations and quality measures
data.cms.gov


CDC WONDER
Mortality and natality data at county level (with suppression for small counts)
wonder.cdc.gov


NHGIS
Historical and current Census/ACS data with pre-joined geography, standardized across decades
nhgis.org

URL

mrlc.gov

Landsat, Sentinel-2, elevation, and other satellite imagery

Daily and monthly temperature and precipitation grids for the U.S.
prism.oregonstate.edu

Heat vulnerability and heat health risk products
heat.gov

Air quality monitoring data
aqs.epa.gov


Microsoft Planetary Computer
Cloud-hosted satellite imagery and environmental data (Landsat, Sentinel, NAIP)
planetarycomputer.microsoft.com

title: Resources
text:
Software


ArcGIS Pro

ArcGIS Pro download and installation
ArcGIS Pro system requirements
ArcGIS Pro documentation
ArcGIS Pro tutorials
Esri Training (free courses)



QGIS

QGIS download — install the Long-Term Release (LTR)
QGIS documentation
QGIS tutorials and tips



Other Useful Tools

GeoDa — free software for exploratory spatial data analysis, Moran’s I, LISA, spatial regression
ColorBrewer — select cartographic color schemes (sequential, diverging, qualitative) that are colorblind-safe and print-friendly
epsg.io — look up coordinate reference systems by EPSG code or name
OpenStreetMap — free, editable world map; useful for basemaps and amenity data
Overpass Turbo — query and download OpenStreetMap data
GTFS feeds — transit schedule data for access analyses

title: Resources
text:
Tutorials and Learning Resources


GIS Fundamentals

Esri Academy (free MOOCs) — Esri’s official training platform with many free courses on ArcGIS Pro
GIS&T Body of Knowledge — comprehensive, peer-reviewed resource on GIS concepts
Bolstad & Manson textbook companion site: gisfundamentals.org



Public Health GIS

CDC GIS resources — GIS applications in public health from CDC
WHO GIS resources — global health mapping resources



Cartography and Visualization

ColorBrewer — essential for choosing map color schemes
Axis Maps Cartography Guide — practical cartographic design advice
